metadata.seed: I was doing okay, but, I mean, I got comfortable with that extra income, and so now my fear is that I've gotten too comfortable with it, and now that it's not there anymore, it's going to affect the fact I'm not going to be able to pay for food or my rent.

patient.query: I was doing okay, but, I mean, I got comfortable with that extra income, and so now my fear is that I've gotten too comfortable with it, and now that it's not there anymore, it's going to affect the fact I'm not going to be able to pay for food or my rent.

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that losing this extra income will leave you unable to pay for food and rent. What evidence do you have that this will happen? Are there other ways you could manage or plan for that possibility?

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, it feels really uncertain right now. I’ve been juggling my expenses for a while, and even the slightest change makes me panic. It’s just hard to think about planning when everything feels so tight already. I guess I just keep worrying that any small setback could spiral out of control.

llm_response.response: It sounds like the thought that a tiny change could spiral into a big crisis is weighing heavily on you. What evidence do you have that this will actually happen? Have there been moments when a small shift didn’t lead to a disaster? Looking at those times might help you see a different perspective.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there have been times when things worked out, even when I thought they wouldn’t. Like that time my car needed repairs, and I managed to figure it out without too much trouble. But, it’s hard to hold onto those moments when the fear of losing control feels so real. I just... I don't want to be in a position where I can’t handle it. It feels like I’m walking on a tightrope.

llm_response.response: I hear how unsettling that sense of losing control feels, especially when it seems so close to your everyday life. It’s understandable that those moments when things still work out feel fleeting and hard to keep in mind. You’re not alone in feeling like you’re walking a tightrope, and it’s okay to acknowledge that tension.

patient.query: Thanks for saying that. I guess I just don’t want to let anyone down, you know? There’s this pressure to always have everything figured out, to be the one who can handle it all. But inside, it feels like I’m just holding on by a thread. It’s exhausting to feel like I’m in this constant state of worry about what’s next.

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel a lot of pressure to be the one who always has things under control. What evidence do you have that you need to be perfect to avoid letting others down? Have you noticed times when you were okay even if things weren’t fully figured out?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I mean, I guess I grew up with this expectation that I had to succeed, you know? It’s like my family always praised the "doers," and if you stumbled, it was tough. But it’s hard to shake off that feeling, even now. Sometimes I see people around me struggling, and I just think I can’t let that happen to me. It feels like a responsibility, like if I don’t keep it together, everything will fall apart. It’s just heavy.

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure to always succeed has become a heavy weight you carry, especially when you see others struggling and feel you must protect yourself from that same fate. That sense of responsibility can feel overwhelming, almost as if everything hinges on your own performance. It’s understandable that this expectation would linger and make it hard to let go of the fear of failure.

patient.query: Yeah, that really resonates. It’s like I constantly have this voice in my head telling me that if I slip up, it’ll all come crashing down. I don’t want to be a burden or end up in a position where I can’t take care of myself. It just makes me feel so anxious, and then I get stuck in this cycle of worrying that I’m not doing enough, even when I’m trying my best. It’s a lot.

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that inner voice feels, and it’s understandable that it can make you feel stuck. What evidence do you have that a slip‑up would truly bring everything crashing down, and how often does that actually happen? Maybe we could explore a more balanced view—what might happen if you made a mistake but still managed to recover or learn from it?

patient.query: I really feel that. There's this voice that keeps telling me that if I mess up, everything will just fall apart. I don’t want to be a burden or find myself in a situation where I can’t handle things. It’s so overwhelming, and then I just spiral into worrying that I’m not doing enough, even when I know I'm trying my hardest. It feels heavy and exhausting.

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the worry that a mistake could bring everything crashing down feels like a heavy, exhausting pressure. What evidence do you have that a slip-up would actually cause that kind of collapse, and have you seen moments where a mistake happened but things still stayed manageable? Thinking about those times might help you see that the outcome isn’t always as dire as it feels.

patient.query: Yeah, when you put it that way, I can think of a couple of times where things didn’t blow up. Like, I had that late payment on a bill once, and while I was freaking out about it, it didn’t ruin my life or anything. But still, it’s hard to shake off that feeling that one mistake could lead to something bigger. I guess the worry just takes over sometimes, and I forget about those moments when things turned out okay. It’s like my mind

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea that one slip could spiral out of control is weighing heavily on you. What evidence do you have that a single mistake would actually lead to something catastrophic? How might you view the situation if you imagined that worst‑case scenario—what would it look like, and how likely does that feel to you?

patient.query: Yeah, I can think of a couple of times where things didn’t blow up. Like, I had that late payment on a bill once, and even though I was freaking out, it didn’t ruin my life. But still, it’s hard to shake off that feeling that one mistake could lead to something bigger. The worry just takes over sometimes, and I forget those moments when things turned out okay. It’s just hard to trust that it'll be fine again.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense that any slip could spiral out of control, even though you’ve seen that’s not always the case. What evidence do you have that a single mistake will inevitably lead to disaster? Maybe we can explore other ways to view those moments—what might a friend say about the situation?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel heavy. I guess when I think of mistakes, I recall times when it felt like everything just piled up. Like, one late payment led to another, and the stress made it hard to focus on solutions. But, if I think about it, I have friends who’ve messed up financially and bounced back, and they always remind me that it’s not the end of the world. Still, it’s hard to convince myself of that when I’m in the thick of it

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy it feels when those mistakes pile up. You mentioned that friends have bounced back—what does that suggest about your own ability to recover? How might you use that perspective to shift the feeling that this is the end of the world?
